narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your academic journey, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.40, LSAT score of 30, upper-middle family income background, part-time enrollment plans, and school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3. The admissions model, trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicted a 56% chance that you would fail it on your first try—crossing the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to succeed on that critical test. This prediction wasn't random; it hinged on a few key aspects of your profile that stood out most strongly to the model. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers that year—far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average around the 5th or 6th decile—and signaled potential challenges in sustaining strong performance heading into law school. On a more positive note, your Year 1 decile of 4 worked in your favor, pulling the prediction somewhat toward passing since it showed you were in the lower-middle range early on, better than many but not elite compared to the average of about 6. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 2.40 nudged things toward success, as it indicated some foundational capability despite being below the dataset's average of 3.21. These top three elements—your later-year grades being the dominant concern—overrode other details like your LSAT or part-time status, which are less common but didn't sway the outcome enough here. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, who often maintain consistent mid-to-high deciles and stronger GPAs throughout, your profile showed a dip in momentum that raised red flags for long-term success. While factors like your family income bracket aligned with many admitted students, they couldn't counterbalance the academic trajectory concerns. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Hispanic man with clear drive to enter law—many in similar backgrounds overcome these hurdles—but the model saw higher risk based on patterns from past bar-takers. To move forward, focus on bolstering your recent academic record, perhaps through postgraduate courses or a higher LSAT retake to demonstrate upward trends. Consider full-time programs if feasible, as part-time paths are rarer and may add unseen risks. Reach out to admissions for feedback or reapply after targeted improvements; your story shows resilience worth building on.
